skill.name: patient-recruitment-ad-gen
skill.description: Generate ethical, compliant, and patient-friendly recruitment advertisements for clinical trials.
skill.summary: Generate IRB/EC-compliant patient recruitment advertisements for clinical trials with plain-language summaries, eligibility criteria, and ethics statements.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: Evaluated 64 static findings across 7 files (1499 lines). All high-severity static findings are false positives: 38 backtick detections are markdown code formatting, 10 cryptographic-algorithm detections are text substrings in regulatory documents and JSON metadata, 8 system-reconnaissance detections are medical/regulatory text content. The Python script uses only standard library imports with no network, file I/O, subprocess, or dynamic code execution. Two minor findings remain: a hardcoded GitHub source URL and an example command path referencing a hidden directory. No prompt injection, data exfiltration, or malicious intent found.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded source URL
low_findings.description: A hardcoded GitHub URL is present at SKILL.md:7 for source attribution. This is not a data exfiltration risk since the URL points to the skill's own repository and no sensitive data is transmitted. Confidence: 0.15 (very low). The URL is a standard source-reference link for a GitHub-hosted skill and follows marketplace conventions.

low_findings.title: Example command references hidden directory path
low_findings.description: An example CLI invocation at SKILL.md:159 uses a path containing the .openclaw hidden directory (/Users/z04030865/.openclaw/workspace/...). This is an example command showing how to run the script, not actual hidden file access. The path is a placeholder from the skill author's development environment. Confidence: 0.10 (very low). No actual hidden file enumeration or access occurs.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown backtick code formatting flagged as shell execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The static analyzer detected 38 instances of Ruby/shell backtick execution across SKILL.md (lines 22-280) and references/template_examples.md (lines 5-214). All are markdown backtick characters used for code block formatting and inline code styling. These are standard markdown syntax, not shell command execution. No actual subprocess, os.system, or eval calls exist in the codebase. All 38 findings are false positives.

dangerous_patterns.title: Text substrings misidentified as weak cryptographic algorithms
dangerous_patterns.description: The static analyzer flagged 10 locations as using weak cryptographic algorithms at scripts/main.py (lines 27, 34, 232, 242), SKILL.md (lines 3, 24, 98), references/template_examples.md (lines 154, 176), references/ema_guidelines.md (line 33), and the audit result JSON (line 62). These are false positives triggered by substrings like 'sha' appearing in words such as 'research' and 'Phase', or by SHA256 hash fields in JSON metadata. The Python script only performs string formatting and has no cryptographic operations. All 10 findings are false positives.

dangerous_patterns.title: Regulatory text misidentified as system reconnaissance
dangerous_patterns.description: The static analyzer flagged 8 locations as system reconnaissance at SKILL.md (lines 58, 70, 204, 248), references/fda_guidance.md (line 53), references/ich_gcp.md (line 122), references/template_examples.md (lines 292, 295), and the audit result JSON (lines 190, 225). These are false positives triggered by keywords like 'who', 'host', 'uname' appearing in clinical/regulatory documentation text. No hostname, username, or system enumeration commands exist. All 8 findings are false positives.

dangerous_patterns.title: Security checklist items misidentified as path traversal
dangerous_patterns.description: The static analyzer flagged path traversal sequences at SKILL.md lines 225 and 228. These lines are part of a security checklist that explicitly warns against path traversal: 'No unauthorized file system access (../)' and 'Input file paths validated (no ../ traversal)'. These are defensive security controls documented as requirements, not actual path traversal attempts. Both findings are false positives.

content.user_title: Generate Compliant Clinical Trial Recruitment Ads
content.value_statement: Writing IRB/EC-compliant patient recruitment materials is time-consuming and requires knowledge of FDA, EMA, and ICH-GCP regulations. This skill automates the creation of ethical, plain-language recruitment advertisements that meet regulatory standards while being accessible to potential participants.
actual_capabilities: Generates structured patient recruitment advertisements with headline, study summary, eligibility criteria, procedures, risks and benefits, and contact information
Produces plain-language content at a 6th-8th grade reading level for broad patient accessibility
Includes mandatory IRB/EC compliance elements such as voluntary participation statements and confidentiality protections
Supports multiple output formats including formatted text for print and structured JSON for programmatic use
Provides reference templates for oncology, diabetes, cardiology, and pediatric therapeutic areas
Validates required input parameters before generating output and rejects incomplete requests
limitations: Cannot review or approve content for regulatory compliance; all output must be reviewed by an IRB/EC before distribution
Requires all input parameters to be provided explicitly; does not infer or guess missing study details
Generates templates based on provided inputs only; does not verify accuracy of clinical information against a study protocol
Output is limited to English language recruitment materials

use_cases.title: Clinical Research Coordinator
use_cases.description: A research coordinator at an academic medical center needs to quickly produce IRB-compliant recruitment flyers for a new Phase II diabetes trial. They provide study parameters and receive a formatted ad ready for IRB submission.
use_cases.target_user: Clinical research coordinators and study site staff

use_cases.title: Contract Research Organization
use_cases.description: A CRO managing multiple clinical trials needs consistent, compliant recruitment materials across different sites and therapeutic areas. They use the skill to generate standardized ads from protocol specifications.
use_cases.target_user: CRO project managers and clinical operations teams

use_cases.title: Principal Investigator
use_cases.description: A principal investigator launching a new study needs to review and approve patient-facing recruitment language. They generate a draft advertisement, verify all compliance elements are present, and submit to the IRB with confidence.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a basic recruitment ad
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a patient recruitment advertisement for a Phase II clinical trial studying an investigational oral medication for Type 2 Diabetes. Target population is adults 18-65. Study duration is 12 weeks with 6 clinic visits. Site is City Medical Center. PI is Dr. Sarah Chen. Contact: (555) 123-4567. IRB reference: IRB-2024-001.
prompt_templates.scenario: Basic ad generation with standard input parameters

prompt_templates.title: Generate an ad with compensation details
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a recruitment flyer for a Phase III vaccine study for children ages 6 months to 5 years. Include compensation of $75 per visit for time and travel. Study duration is 12 months with 6 visits. Ensure all IRB-required elements are present including voluntary participation and confidentiality statements.
prompt_templates.scenario: Pediatric study recruitment with participant compensation

prompt_templates.title: Generate a recruitment ad for social media
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a short patient recruitment advertisement suitable for social media posts for an oncology Phase II immunotherapy trial. Keep the language patient-friendly and concise while maintaining all required compliance elements. The study is for adults with advanced cancer who have progressed after standard treatment.

prompt_templates.title: Generate multiple ad variants for IRB review
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate three variations of a recruitment advertisement for a Phase I cardiac device study. Each variation should use different headlines and phrasing while keeping all compliance elements identical. Target population is adults 21-75 with documented arrhythmia. Include a comparison table showing which compliance elements appear in each variant.
prompt_templates.scenario: A/B testing variants for IRB submission with compliance tracking

output_examples.input: Generate a recruitment ad for a Phase II Type 2 Diabetes study with oral medication, adults 18-65, 12 weeks, at City Medical Center
output_examples.output: A formatted recruitment advertisement containing: a headline stating 'Research Study for Type 2 Diabetes: Volunteers Needed', an about section explaining the research purpose in plain language, eligibility criteria describing who may qualify, a what-is-involved section detailing the time commitment and procedures, a risks and benefits section with balanced disclosure, a your-rights section confirming voluntary participation and confidentiality, and contact information with PI name, location, phone, and IRB reference number.

output_examples.input: Generate a recruitment flyer for a pediatric vaccine trial with compensation information
output_examples.output: A structured recruitment flyer with all standard compliance sections plus a compensation disclosure stating the per-visit payment amount and total, with a note that details will be provided during informed consent. The ad uses age-appropriate language for parents/guardians and includes the required statement that participation is voluntary and will not affect the child's regular medical care.

best_practices: Always provide all required input parameters including disease condition, study phase, intervention type, target population, duration, location, PI name, contact info, and IRB reference number
Review all generated content against your approved study protocol before submitting to the IRB/EC for review and approval
Use the plain-language output as-is for patient-facing materials; the 6th-8th grade reading level is calibrated for broad accessibility
anti_patterns: Distributing recruitment materials without IRB/EC review and approval, even if the generated content appears compliant
Modifying the ethics statements, voluntary participation language, or risk disclosures in the generated output without IRB/EC approval
Using the generated advertisement as a substitute for the full informed consent document rather than as a recruitment tool

faq.question: Does this skill guarantee IRB/EC approval of recruitment materials?
faq.answer: No. This skill generates drafts that include required compliance elements based on FDA, EMA, and ICH-GCP guidelines. All recruitment materials must be reviewed and approved by your IRB/EC before use.

faq.question: What clinical trial phases are supported?
faq.answer: The skill supports Phase I through Phase IV clinical trials. Each phase has tailored language describing the research appropriately, from early safety evaluation to post-approval studies.

faq.question: Can the skill generate ads in languages other than English?
faq.answer: The current version generates English-language recruitment materials only. Translations should be prepared separately and submitted to the IRB/EC for review.

faq.question: How is participant compensation handled in the generated ads?
faq.answer: Compensation is an optional input parameter. When provided, the skill includes a disclosure stating the amount is for time and travel, with details to be provided during informed consent. Compensation is never emphasized as the primary benefit.

faq.question: What reading level are the generated ads written at?
faq.answer: The skill targets a 6th-8th grade reading level to ensure accessibility for a broad patient population, consistent with NIH Plain Language guidelines and informed consent best practices.

faq.question: Can I use the generated ads on social media?
faq.answer: Yes. The output format is suitable for print, digital, and social media distribution. However, social media ads may need to be shortened while retaining all required compliance elements, and must still receive IRB/EC approval before posting.
